sound_type: escalating blood-curdling shrieks -- full scream sequence: escalating from hoarse hoarse onset through multiple phases to prolonged full-force shriek; disturbingly human-like timbre; primary frequency 300-3,000 Hz with most alarming components 500-2,000 Hz; duration 2-8 seconds per scream; sequences may continue for minutes; involuntary human fear response documented in wildlife literature
ecosystem: Forest -- mature and old-growth Pacific Northwest forest; all elevations sea level to subalpine; wherever deer are present; secretive, resident, primarily crepuscular and nocturnal
season: Resident (year-round; full scream primarily reproductive context: female estrus and male response; also territorial and stress response; not season-limited)
conservation_status: Least Concern (IUCN). Washington State population estimated 1,500-2,500. Managed as game species. Secretive; encounters rare relative to population density. Apex predator.
milestone: TIES SPS MAXIMUM. E=9. First of three consecutive E=9 entries (302-304). The SPS engine's absolute ceiling held for three consecutive degrees for the first time. The most terrifying vocalization in the Pacific Northwest forest.

coordinates.description: Widespread throughout Pacific Northwest. Cascades, Olympic Peninsula, Coast Range, all forested regions with deer. Washington State: 1,500-2,500 cougars. Territory 50-150 square miles. Most widely distributed large felid in Western Hemisphere.

acoustic_data.vocalization_type: Vocal -- larynx-produced escalating screams. Primary context: reproductive (female estrus) and territorial. Real and documented vocalization verified in wildlife agency reports, park service records, acoustic databases.
acoustic_data.mechanics: Escalating structure: hoarse onset builds through multiple phases to full-force prolonged shriek. The escalation is the defining quality -- the worst moment arrives last. Harmonic structure overlaps with human screaming frequency range.
acoustic_data.frequency_range: 300-3,000 Hz primary. Most alarming components 500-2,000 Hz (peak human hearing sensitivity). Harmonic overlap with human screaming produces 'disturbingly human-like' quality documented in wildlife literature.
acoustic_data.duration: 2-8 seconds per individual scream. Sequences in reproductive context: minutes, multiple nights.
acoustic_data.human_response: Documented: rapid heart rate, involuntary freezing, flight urge, source localization failure. Response persists in individuals with prior knowledge -- intellectual identification does not override somatic response. Multiple wildlife agency and researcher records.
acoustic_data.e9_calibration: E=9: maximum SPS calibration. Three simultaneous properties no other PNW vocalization matches: (1) disturbingly human-like timbre triggering maximum threat-recognition response; (2) escalating structure reaching maximum intensity after onset; (3) clinically documented fear response in trained wildlife workers. SPS ceiling.

nodes.title: E=9 -- Disturbingly Human-Like Timbre
nodes.description: The cougar scream exploits the brain's calibration to respond to human screaming as a priority threat signal. The harmonic overlap between the cougar's vocal apparatus and human screaming frequency range produces a sound that triggers maximum threat-recognition response regardless of listener knowledge. This is the property that justifies E=9.

nodes.title: Escalating Structure -- The Worst Moment Arrives Last
nodes.description: Most alarming sounds are startling at onset; the cougar scream is most alarming at its peak, which arrives after the escalation. Hoarse onset building through multiple phases to full-force prolonged shriek. Duration 2-8 seconds. The sustained escalation is what makes the sound qualitatively different from a startling onset sound.

nodes.title: Documented Fear Response in Wildlife Workers
nodes.description: The involuntary fear response to the cougar scream persists in trained wildlife workers who know intellectually that they are hearing a cougar. Rapid heart rate, freezing, flight urge, source localization failure. Clinical documentation in WDFW encounter records and wildlife research literature. The somatic response bypasses intellectual processing.

nodes.title: Predator-Prey Sequence at Degrees 301-302
nodes.description: Roosevelt Elk (SPS 301, E=8, Cervidae) followed by Cougar (SPS 302, E=9, Felidae). Prey species documented at E=8; primary predator at E=9. Ecologically correct consecutive degree sequence: the elk announces itself; the predator follows. Olympic Peninsula co-occurrence in September-October.

cross_references.relationship: Previous degree. E=8. Primary cougar prey species at acoustic extreme. Prey-predator consecutive sequence. Olympic Peninsula co-occurrence.
Next degree. E=9. Second consecutive max. FIRST CANIDAE. Pack predator competing with cougar for similar prey.
E=9. Third consecutive max. Closes three-consecutive-E=9 apex predator arc.
E=6 + E=9 = E=15. Crystalline melodic grunge pairs with blood-curdling forest scream. Craft vs. primal maximum.
